text: The robotic surgical system is used to lift the sigmoid colon, exposing the blood vessel supply. The surgeon creates a dissection plane between the mesentery blood supply and the retroperitoneum, moving from the medial to the lateral side. The ureter, highlighted with green dye, is visible and carefully avoided during dissection. The vascular pedicle is isolated.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer device to coagulate and cut the blood vessel supply to the sigmoid colon. The dissection continues from the medial to the lateral side, avoiding the ureter, which is visible with green dye. A Raytech gauze is used to push on the medial side, revealing the dissection plane as the surgeon mobilizes the left colon, freeing it from the abdominal sidewall and retroperitoneum. The dissection is carried distally, ensuring the uterus is identified and avoided, freeing the sigmoid colon from its lateral attachments.

The surgeon identifies two duplicated ureters on the left side using green dye and Firefly mode, observing their tubular structure and urine passage. The dissection continues on the distal sigmoid colon, creating a plane between the mesentery and retroperitoneum. The robotic instrument allows the camera to be driven underneath the colon, providing clear visualization of the ureters and facilitating precise dissection.

The surgeon identifies the distal margin for sigmoid resection and divides the mesentery using a vessel sealer device. ICG dye is injected to assess blood flow, marking the colon for resection. A stapler device is introduced to staple the distal sigmoid colon above the rectum. A small incision is made to extract the specimen, and the anvil of the stapler is placed in the proximal colon. An EEA stapler is introduced through the rectum, mating the anvil ends to ensure straight orientation. ICG dye is injected again to confirm perfusion, and the stapler is fired to complete the anastomosis.

The surgeon disengages the stapler and checks the anastomosis for tension, ensuring it is straight. A leak test is performed by filling the pelvis with fluid and introducing a scope to conduct an air leak test, confirming there is no leak at the anastomosis.
